findings.evidence: 2 unexpected SUID binary(ies) found out of 17 total
findings.details:
SUID binaries run with elevated privileges and are common privilege escalation targets. The following SUID binaries are not in the expected list:
  - /usr/lib/xorg/Xorg.wrap
  - /usr/libexec/spice-client-glib-usb-acl-helper
Review each binary and remove the SUID bit if not needed: chmod u-s <file>

findings.evidence: 1 sensitive file(s) with overly permissive permissions
findings.details:
Sensitive configuration files have permissions that are too loose. This may allow unauthorized users to read credentials or private keys:
  - /etc/ssl/private: 0o710 (should be 0o700 or stricter)

Permission issues found in /var/www/html:
  - /var/www/html: group-writable and world-executable (0o2775)
  - /var/www/html/alfred/.env: world-readable (0o644), may contain credentials
  - /var/www/html/wordpress/wp-config.php: world-readable (0o775), may contain credentials
  - /var/www/html/eqmon/api/config.php: world-readable (0o644), may contain credentials
  - /var/www/html/eqmon/.htaccess: world-readable (0o755), may contain credentials
  - /var/www/html/ecoeye_alert_relay/public_html/.htaccess: world-readable (0o664), may contain credentials
  - /var/www/html/wordpress/.htaccess: world-readable (0o775), may contain credentials
Ensure web files are owned by www-data with minimal permissions (644 for files, 755 for directories).

findings.evidence: No active firewall detected (checked ufw, nftables, iptables)
findings.details: No firewall is active on this system. All ports are exposed to the network. Enable a firewall immediately. On Ubuntu/Debian: 'sudo ufw enable'. Note: if this is an AWS instance, security groups provide network-level filtering but host-based firewall is still recommended for defense in depth.

findings.evidence: PermitRootLogin not explicitly set in sshd_config
findings.details: PermitRootLogin is not explicitly configured. Modern OpenSSH defaults to 'prohibit-password', but explicit configuration is recommended.
Password-based SSH authentication is enabled. This exposes the server to brute-force attacks. Disable PasswordAuthentication and use key-based authentication exclusively.

findings.evidence: Protocol directive not set (modern OpenSSH defaults to 2 only)
findings.details: The Protocol directive is absent, which is correct for modern OpenSSH (>= 7.4) where Protocol 1 support has been removed.

findings.evidence: No weak ciphers, MACs, or KEX algorithms found in sshd_config
findings.details: SSH cryptographic configuration does not include known weak algorithms. Note: if ciphers/MACs/KEX are not explicitly set, OpenSSH defaults apply.
MaxAuthTries is not explicitly configured. OpenSSH defaults to 6, which allows more brute-force attempts per connection than recommended. Set MaxAuthTries to 3 or lower.
